1: 'Woe to the shepherds who destroy and scatter the sheep of my pasture!' The woe-oracle against the false shepherds opens the chapter that ends with the Righteous Branch. Jesus is the Good Shepherd (John 10:11-14) who stands in contrast to every false shepherd in Israel's history. His self-identification as the good shepherd takes up the entire language of Jeremiah 23: he knows his sheep, they know him, he lays down his life for them rather than scattering and destroying them.
2: The shepherds have scattered YHWH's flock and driven them away — YHWH will punish them for it. Jesus identifies himself as the one YHWH sends in fulfillment of this promise of punishment and replacement. In the Parable of the Tenants (Matthew 21:33-46) the wicked tenants-shepherds are removed and the vineyard given to others. The punishment of the false shepherds is YHWH's vindication of the flock.
3: 'I myself will gather the remnant of my flock from all the lands where I have driven them.' YHWH as the gathering shepherd. Jesus: 'I have other sheep that are not of this sheep pen; I must bring them also' (John 10:16). His gathering work extends beyond ethnic Israel to the nations — the global gathering that 'all the lands' implies. Matthew 28:19-20: the Great Commission is the mechanism by which YHWH gathers the global remnant through the Son's sent messengers.
4: 'I will raise up shepherds who will truly care for them; they will no longer be afraid or terrified, and none will be missing.' The promise of faithful under-shepherds. Jesus fulfills the 'none missing' (literally: 'they shall not be lacking') guarantee: 'of all those he has given me I will lose none, but raise them up at the last day' (John 6:39); 'I give them eternal life, and they shall never perish; no one will snatch them out of my hand' (John 10:28). The 'none missing' of Jeremiah 23:4 is the 'lose none' of John 6:39.
5: The Righteous Branch Oracle: hineh yamim baim neum YHWH vahaqimoti leDavid tzemach tzaddiq: Behold, the days are coming when I will raise up for David a righteous Branch who will reign wisely and do what is just and right in the land. This is the central messianic promise of Jeremiah. Tzemach (Branch/Shoot) is a technical messianic title: it appears again at Jeremiah 33:15, Zechariah 3:8 ('my servant the Branch'), and Zechariah 6:12 ('the man whose name is the Branch, and he will branch out from his place and build the temple of the LORD'). The Branch reigns wisely (vesakhal), does justice and righteousness (mishpat utzedaqah) — the defining characteristics of the ideal Davidic king (Isaiah 9:7; Jeremiah 11:1-5). Jesus fulfills every element: Davidic descent (Luke 1:27, 32-33; Matthew 1:1), wisdom (Luke 2:52; Matthew 12:42), justice (Matthew 12:18-21 citing Isaiah 42:1-4), and righteousness (Hebrews 7:26).
6: YHWH Tsidqenu: 'This is the name by which he will be called: The LORD our Righteousness.' The divine name given to the messianic king is itself a form of YHWH's name — the Branch is identified as YHWH present. This is one of the most explicit OT identifications of the messianic king with YHWH himself. Paul applies this directly to Christ: 'Christ Jesus... has become for us wisdom from God — that is, our righteousness, holiness and redemption' (1 Corinthians 1:30); 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Corinthians 5:21). Jesus is YHWH Tsidqenu — the LORD who becomes our righteousness.
7: The new Exodus that will supersede the old: 'no longer will they say, As the LORD lives who brought Israel up from Egypt.' The greater deliverance will make the first deliverance seem secondary by comparison. Jesus inaugurates this new Exodus: Matthew 2:15 ('Out of Egypt I called my son') identifies Jesus as the true Israel making the definitive Exodus; the transfiguration has Moses and Elijah speaking about his 'departure' (exodon, Luke 9:31) — his death-and-resurrection is the new Exodus; the Last Supper reframes Passover around his own body and blood as the new Exodus meal (Luke 22:14-20).
8: 'As the LORD lives who brought up the descendants of Israel from the north country and from all the lands where he had driven them.' The new Exodus includes the diaspora from all directions — a global gathering. Acts 2:5-11 shows this beginning at Pentecost: Jews 'from every nation under heaven' hear the gospel. The gathering from north and south and all the lands is the Spirit-enabled gathering of the new covenant era.
9: Jeremiah's anguish at the prophets' corruption — his heart broken, his bones trembling, like a drunken man overcome by YHWH's holy words. This prophetic agony participates in the divine grief. Jesus's agony in Gethsemane (Luke 22:44: 'being in anguish, he prayed more earnestly') is the Son bearing the same weight of the holy word of judgment — but taking it into himself rather than pronouncing it outward.
10: Land full of adulterers; curse making the land mourn; the wilderness pastures dried up. The cosmic consequences of moral corruption: the land itself responds to human faithlessness (Romans 8:19-22). Jesus's death and resurrection begins the reversal: the creation that was subjected to futility will be liberated (Romans 8:21). His resurrection-presence transforms the barren (John 15:5: 'apart from me you can do nothing'; 'with me you bear much fruit').
11: 'Both prophet and priest are godless; even in my own house I have found their wickedness.' The corruption at the center — the sacred professions have been profaned. Jesus drives out the merchants from the temple (Matthew 21:12-13) precisely because the house of prayer has been corrupted by those who should have been its guardians. His action enacts this oracle's judgment in real time.
12: Slippery paths in darkness for the false prophets — they will be driven into darkness and fall. Jesus's 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12; Jeremiah 9:5) is the answer to the darkness in which the false prophets stumble. Those who follow him 'will never walk in darkness but will have the light of life.' The slippery path in the dark is the condition of those who reject the true shepherd-prophet.
13: Prophets of Samaria prophesying by Baal, leading Israel astray. The northern kingdom's false prophecy as the precursor to Jerusalem's worse version (Jeremiah 23:14). Jesus's warning about false prophets (Matthew 7:15-23; Jeremiah 24:11, 24) anticipates the NT pattern. The test of the true prophet: 'by their fruit you will recognize them' (Matthew 7:20) — not the claims made but the direction they lead the flock (toward or away from the true shepherd).
14: 'In the prophets of Jerusalem I have seen something even more horrible: they commit adultery and live by lies.' Greater privilege means greater accountability. Jesus's woes against Chorazin and Bethsaida (Matthew 11:21-22): 'it will be more bearable for Tyre and Sidon on the day of judgment than for you' — those who received the most (miracles in the presence of Jesus) and rejected it bear greater judgment.
15: Wormwood and poisoned water for the false prophets. The bitter cup they cause others to drink returns to them. Jesus takes the bitter cup that judgment requires (Mark 14:36: 'take this cup from me — yet not what I will, but what you will'). His willingness to drink the cup of judgment means that those who trust in him are not given the wormwood-and-gall that the false prophets prescribed as safe.
16: 'Do not listen to the prophets who are prophesying to you; they are filling you with false hopes.' The prophets who speak from their own hearts (machzon libbam). Jesus's teaching comes from the Father, not from his own heart in isolation: 'my teaching is not my own; it comes from the one who sent me' (John 7:16); 'the words I say to you I do not speak on my own authority; the Father, living in me, is doing his work' (John 14:10). The criterion for distinguishing true from false prophecy is whether it originates in the divine council or the prophet's own imagination.
17: 'They keep telling those who follow stubbornness of heart: No disaster will come.' The false peace — confirming the stubborn in their stubbornness. Jesus refuses this comfort: 'do not think I came to bring peace to the earth; I did not come to bring peace, but a sword' (Matthew 10:34). The sword that divides is more merciful than the false peace that confirms people in paths that end in judgment.
18: 'For which of them has stood in the LORD's council to see and hear his word?' The sod YHWH (divine council) — the criterion for authentic prophecy. Jesus has unique, permanent standing in the Father's council: 'the Son can do nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing, because whatever the Father does the Son also does' (John 5:19); 'I have made known to you everything I heard from my Father' (John 15:15). He is the prophet who has fully stood in YHWH's council and speaks from complete divine intimacy.
19: The storm of YHWH, his wrath going out like a whirling tempest, falling on the heads of the wicked. The divine-warrior storm that no false prophet can prevent. Jesus stills the storm (Matthew 8:23-27; Mark 4:35-41; Luke 8:22-25) — 'who is this? Even the wind and the waves obey him!' — demonstrating that he is YHWH the storm-commander, not a victim of storms. The storm of divine wrath that falls on the wicked falls on him at the cross so it need not fall on those who are in him.
20: 'In the latter days you will understand it clearly.' The prophetic word intelligible only after the fact. Luke 24:45: 'then he opened their minds so they could understand the Scriptures.' The risen Jesus is the interpreter of the prophetic word — in the latter days, standing with the disciples after his resurrection, he makes the purposes of YHWH's heart clear. The disciples are the 'latter days' generation to whom Jeremiah's prophecy finally becomes intelligible.
21: 'I did not send these prophets, yet they ran; I did not speak to them, yet they prophesied.' The unsent, self-authorized prophets. Jesus: 'I was sent by him' (ekeinos me apesteilen) — the 'sent-ness' formula occurs dozens of times in John. His authority derives entirely from the one who sent him (John 8:42; Jeremiah 17:3, 8, 18, 21, 23, 25). The distinction between the authentic sent prophet and the self-running prophet is the distinction between Jesus and every false claimant.
22: 'If they had stood in my council and proclaimed my words, they would have turned them from their evil way.' Authentic prophecy that comes from the divine council actually changes people. Jesus's words have transformative power: 'the words I have spoken to you are spirit and they are life' (John 6:63); 'if you hold to my teaching, you are really my disciples. Then you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free' (John 8:31-32). True prophetic words produce repentance and transformation; false ones confirm existing behavior.
23: 'Am I only a God nearby, declares the LORD, and not a God far away?' The rhetorical question of divine omnipresence. Jesus promises the same omnipresence in his own person: 'I am with you always, to the very end of the age' (Matthew 28:20); 'where two or three gather in my name, there am I with them' (Matthew 18:20). The God who fills heaven and earth is now present 'nearby' in the most intimate sense — in human flesh among his people.
24: 'Can anyone hide in secret places where I cannot see him? Do I not fill heaven and earth?' The omniscient, omnipresent God who fills all space. Jesus speaks with the same authority about his own omniscience: 'he knew all people and needed no one to testify about man, for he himself knew what was in man' (John 2:24-25); 'I am the good shepherd; I know my sheep' (John 10:14). The God who fills heaven and earth has in Jesus 'filled' earth in a new, incarnate way (Ephesians 4:10: 'he who descended is the very one who ascended higher than all the heavens, in order to fill the whole universe').
25: The dream-prophets claiming 'I have dreamed, I have dreamed!' — the subjective, self-generated prophetic content. Jesus's contrast: 'I tell you what I have seen in the Father's presence' (John 8:38); 'my Father has revealed this to you' (Matthew 16:17). The dreamed prophecy vs. the seen-and-revealed prophecy: Jesus's prophetic authority is grounded in direct, unmediated vision of the Father, not in the vagaries of the prophet's own dream-life.
26: 'How long shall there be lies in the heart of these prophets — prophesying the deceit of their own heart?' The chronic condition of self-deception projected outward as prophecy. John 3:19-21: 'this is the verdict: light has come into the world, but people loved darkness instead of light because their deeds were evil.' Jesus's light exposes the self-deceiving heart — not to condemn (John 3:17) but to bring into the light where transformation can occur.
27: The prophets making people forget YHWH's name through their dreams. The name-of-YHWH as the thing at risk. Jesus inverts this: he makes the Father's name known: 'I have revealed you to those whom you gave me' (John 17:6); 'I have made you known to them, and will continue to make you known' (John 17:26). The dream-prophets who cause forgetting of the divine name are countered by the Son who makes the Father permanently known.
28: 'What has straw to do with grain? Let the one who has my word speak it faithfully.' The contrast between straw (the dream-prophet's chaff) and grain (YHWH's genuine word). John 6:35: 'I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me will never go hungry.' Jesus is the grain — the nourishing, substantial word — versus the straw of false prophecy that fills but does not sustain. His word is the bread that gives life to the world (John 6:33).
29: 'Is not my word like fire, declares the LORD, and like a hammer that shatters rock?' The divine word as fire and hammer. Hebrews 4:12: 'the word of God is alive and active, sharper than any double-edged sword, it penetrates even to dividing soul and spirit, joints and marrow; it judges the thoughts and attitudes of the heart.' Jesus is the Word of God (John 1:1) — the fire, hammer, and sharp sword — who both shatters and reconstructs. At the transfiguration his face shines like the sun and his garments become brilliant (Matthew 17:2) — the fire-of-YHWH incarnate.
30: 'I am against the prophets who steal my words from each other.' Plagiarized prophecy — borrowed words passed off as direct revelation. Jesus's word is wholly his own and wholly the Father's: 'I did not speak on my own, but the Father who sent me commanded me to say all that I have spoken' (John 12:49). There is no gap between his word and the Father's — no borrowing, no contamination, no secondhand transmission.
31: 'I am against the prophets who wag their own tongues and say: This is what the LORD declares.' Tongue-wagging prophecy — the physical gesture of the false prophet speaking his own words with YHWH's authority. James 3:5-6: 'the tongue is a small part of the body, but it makes great boasts... it corrupts the whole body.' Jesus's speech is purified of this gap: he speaks what he hears (John 8:26, 28, 40), sees (John 8:38), and receives (John 17:8).
32: 'I am against those who prophesy lying dreams ... who lead my people astray with reckless lies.' The lying dreamers leading the flock astray. Jesus identifies himself as the only reliable leader of the flock: 'I am the gate for the sheep ... all who ever came before me were thieves and robbers' (John 10:7-8). The lying prophets who lead astray are distinguished from the true shepherd by whether the flock is fed or fleeced, led or misled.
33: The massa (burden/oracle) wordplay: when the people ask 'What is the burden of the LORD?' they become themselves the burden — 'you are the burden, and I will throw you off.' Jesus's inversion: 'Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you... my yoke is easy and my burden is light' (Matthew 11:28-30). The massa that crushes becomes the light yoke that rests: the burden-language of Jeremiah 23:33 is explicitly reversed in Jesus's invitation.
34: Punishment of anyone who says 'the burden of the LORD' — prophet, priest, or person. The divine word has been so abused that the formula must be discontinued. Jesus announces a new covenant of speech: his words are his own because they are the Father's own — no formula required, because the speaker is himself the authentic mediator. His teaching 'as one who had authority, and not as their teachers of the law' (Matthew 7:29) bypasses the formula-dependence entirely.
35: The replacement formula: 'What has the LORD answered you?' — asking about response rather than declaring a burden. Jesus models this responsive posture: 'the Son does nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing' (John 5:19). His whole prophetic ministry is one of listening for the Father's answer and declaring it. The disciple's prayer in John 17 shows this: Jesus reports what he has received and transmits it to his disciples.
36: 'You must no longer speak of the burden of the LORD, for the burden is every person's own word.' The word has been so corrupted by misuse that the vocabulary itself must change. Jesus announces a new vocabulary: 'the words I have spoken to you are spirit and they are life' (John 6:63); in him, YHWH's word and the speaker are identical — there is no gap between the formula and the reality. He himself is the Word (John 1:14).
37: The proper question to the prophet: 'What has the LORD answered you?' The disciple's posture of asking rather than presuming. Jesus teaches his disciples to ask: 'ask and it will be given to you, seek and you will find, knock and the door will be opened to you' (Matthew 7:7); 'until now you have not asked for anything in my name; ask and you will receive, and your joy will be complete' (John 16:24). The asking that replaces the false-burden formula.
38: Because they persist in using the forbidden formula despite the command — they will be punished. The persistence of self-willed religious speech after divine instruction is the same disobedience as the false prophecy itself. Jesus's warning: 'not everyone who says to me Lord, Lord will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father' (Matthew 7:21). Religious formula without covenant reality is the persistent sin Jeremiah identifies here.
39: 'I will surely lift you up and fling you away from my presence — you and the city I gave to your ancestors.' The exile as rejection from the divine presence — the ultimate covenant curse. Jesus's most terrible words use the same expulsion language: 'then I will tell them plainly: I never knew you. Away from me, you evildoers!' (Matthew 7:23); 'depart from me, you who are cursed, into the eternal fire' (Matthew 25:41). The flung-away of 23:39 is the eternal consequence Jesus describes — and which he takes upon himself at the cross ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?') so that his people never hear the final version.
40: 'And I will bring on you everlasting disgrace and lasting shame that will not be forgotten.' Permanent disgrace — the opposite of covenant honor. Jesus's resurrection is the permanent reversal of this disgrace: 'looking to Jesus, the pioneer and perfecter of faith, who for the joy set before him endured the cross, scorning its shame, and sat down at the right hand of the throne of God' (Hebrews 12:2). He endured the maximum disgrace and received the maximum honor — and in him those who were disgraced are restored to honor (1 Peter 2:6: 'the one who trusts in him will never be put to shame').
